question_text: In negative feedback mechanism, the effect of a hormone on a target organ:
explanation: Negative feedback is the most common regulatory mechanism in the endocrine system. In this mechanism, the product or effect of a hormone inhibits further secretion of that hormone or its releasing factors. For example, when cortisol levels rise in blood, cortisol inhibits the release of CRH from the hypothalamus and ACTH from the anterior pituitary, thereby reducing further cortisol production. This self-limiting mechanism prevents overproduction of hormones and maintains homeostasis. It works analogously to a thermostat that switches off heating once the desired temperature is reached.

options.text: Stimulates further secretion of the same hormone
Inhibits further secretion of that hormone or its releasing factors
Has no effect on the secretion of the hormone
Causes permanent shutdown of the endocrine gland

elimination_hints.hint: Stimulating further secretion describes positive feedback, not negative feedback. In negative feedback the response opposes or reverses the original stimulus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing positive feedback (amplification) with negative feedback (inhibition)

elimination_hints.hint: If the hormone had no effect on its own secretion, there would be no feedback regulation at all. The endocrine system tightly regulates hormone levels through feedback loops.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming hormones are secreted without any regulatory control

elimination_hints.hint: Negative feedback temporarily reduces secretion, not permanently shuts down the gland. Once hormone levels fall below the set point, the gland resumes secretion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing temporary inhibition with permanent cessation of gland function

question_text: Which of the following is an example of positive feedback in the human body?
explanation: Oxytocin-mediated uterine contractions during childbirth (parturition) are a classic example of positive feedback. During labour, the foetal head presses against the cervix, sending neural signals to the hypothalamus, which stimulates oxytocin release from the posterior pituitary. Oxytocin causes stronger uterine contractions, which push the baby further against the cervix, generating even more nerve signals, leading to more oxytocin release and progressively stronger contractions. This amplifying cycle continues until delivery, when the stimulus (cervical stretching) is removed. Unlike negative feedback, positive feedback amplifies the original stimulus rather than opposing it.

options.text: Cortisol inhibiting ACTH release from the anterior pituitary
High blood glucose stimulating insulin secretion from pancreas
Oxytocin causing stronger uterine contractions leading to more oxytocin release during childbirth
T3/T4 inhibiting TSH release from the anterior pituitary

elimination_hints.hint: Cortisol inhibiting ACTH is a classic example of negative feedback along the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, not positive feedback.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing negative feedback (cortisol inhibiting ACTH) with positive feedback

elimination_hints.hint: High blood glucose stimulating insulin secretion is a normal stimulus-response mechanism. Once glucose is lowered by insulin, insulin secretion decreases — this is negative feedback, not positive feedback.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a simple regulatory response with positive feedback amplification

elimination_hints.hint: T3/T4 inhibiting TSH is the thyroid negative feedback loop. The thyroid hormones suppress their own stimulatory signal, which is the opposite of amplification.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between negative feedback (inhibition) and positive feedback (amplification)

question_text: The hypothalamus regulates the anterior pituitary through the release of:
explanation: The hypothalamus regulates the anterior pituitary gland by secreting releasing hormones (that stimulate) and inhibiting hormones (that suppress) the secretion of various anterior pituitary hormones. These hypothalamic hormones are transported to the anterior pituitary via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system — a specialized network of blood vessels connecting the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary. Examples include TRH (stimulates TSH), CRH (stimulates ACTH), GnRH (stimulates LH and FSH), GHRH (stimulates GH), and somatostatin (inhibits GH). This hierarchical control forms the basis of the hypothalamo-hypophyseal axis.

options.text: Releasing and inhibiting hormones transported via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system
Hormones directly released into systemic circulation without any portal system
Nerve impulses that travel through the optic nerve to the pituitary
Neurotransmitters released directly into the posterior pituitary

elimination_hints.hint: Hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones are not released into the general systemic circulation. They are transported via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system, which ensures a high local concentration at the anterior pituitary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the portal system and assuming hypothalamic hormones travel via general blood circulation

elimination_hints.hint: The optic nerve carries visual information, not hormonal signals. The hypothalamus communicates with the anterior pituitary via hormones (chemical signalling), not via nerve impulses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing neural pathways for vision with hormonal communication pathways

elimination_hints.hint: The posterior pituitary receives direct neural connections (axons) from the hypothalamus for ADH and oxytocin release. However, the anterior pituitary is regulated by releasing/inhibiting hormones via the portal system, not by direct neurotransmission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the neural regulation of the posterior pituitary with the hormonal regulation of the anterior pituitary

question_text: In the thyroid feedback loop, when blood levels of T3 and T4 increase above normal, they inhibit the secretion of:
explanation: The thyroid feedback loop operates as follows: The hypothalamus secretes TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone), which stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone). TSH acts on the thyroid gland to produce T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine). When blood levels of T3 and T4 rise above the set point, they exert negative feedback on both the hypothalamus (reducing TRH secretion) and the anterior pituitary (reducing TSH secretion). This dual-level inhibition ensures that thyroid hormone production is precisely regulated. When T3/T4 levels fall, the inhibition is removed and TRH/TSH secretion increases again.

options.text: Only TRH from the hypothalamus
Only TSH from the anterior pituitary
GnRH from the hypothalamus and FSH from the anterior pituitary
Both TRH from the hypothalamus and TSH from the anterior pituitary

elimination_hints.hint: T3/T4 inhibit TRH at the hypothalamus, but they also directly inhibit TSH release at the anterior pituitary. The feedback operates at both levels simultaneously.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming negative feedback only acts at one level of the axis

elimination_hints.hint: T3/T4 inhibit TSH at the anterior pituitary, but they also inhibit TRH at the hypothalamus. Ignoring the hypothalamic level gives an incomplete picture of thyroid feedback.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overlooking the hypothalamic component of thyroid negative feedback

elimination_hints.hint: GnRH and FSH belong to the gonadal axis, not the thyroid axis. T3/T4 do not inhibit GnRH or FSH. The thyroid axis involves TRH and TSH.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the thyroid feedback axis with the gonadal feedback axis

question_text: Insulin and glucagon are antagonistic hormones that regulate blood glucose levels. When blood glucose rises after a meal, the response is:
explanation: Insulin and glucagon are antagonistic hormones produced by the endocrine pancreas (Islets of Langerhans) that work together to maintain blood glucose homeostasis. When blood glucose rises after a meal (hyperglycaemia), the beta cells of the pancreatic islets secrete insulin. Insulin promotes glucose uptake by cells (especially liver and muscle), glycogenesis (conversion of glucose to glycogen in liver and muscles), and lipogenesis. These actions lower blood glucose back to normal. Conversely, when blood glucose falls (hypoglycaemia), alpha cells secrete glucagon, which promotes glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, raising blood glucose. This antagonistic regulation is a form of feedback mechanism.

options.text: Beta cells secrete insulin, which promotes glucose uptake and glycogenesis, lowering blood glucose
Alpha cells secrete glucagon, which promotes glycogenolysis, raising blood glucose further
Beta cells secrete glucagon, which promotes glycogenesis
Alpha cells secrete insulin, which inhibits glucose uptake

elimination_hints.hint: Glucagon is secreted when blood glucose is LOW, not high. When blood glucose rises after a meal, glucagon secretion is suppressed. Glucagon raises blood glucose, which would worsen hyperglycaemia.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing when glucagon vs. insulin is secreted in response to blood glucose changes

elimination_hints.hint: This contains two errors: glucagon is secreted by alpha cells (not beta cells), and glucagon promotes glycogenolysis (glycogen breakdown), not glycogenesis (glycogen synthesis).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the cell types and functions of insulin and glucagon

elimination_hints.hint: Insulin is secreted by beta cells, not alpha cells, and insulin promotes glucose uptake, not inhibits it. Alpha cells produce glucagon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the cell types that produce insulin and glucagon

question_text: The hypothalamus is considered the 'integration center' of the endocrine system because it:
explanation: The hypothalamus serves as the integration center linking the nervous system and the endocrine system. It receives neural inputs from various parts of the brain (including the limbic system, cerebral cortex, and sensory pathways) and hormonal signals from the blood (detecting levels of circulating hormones, glucose, temperature, etc.). Based on this integrated information, it secretes releasing and inhibiting hormones that control the anterior pituitary and sends neural signals to the posterior pituitary. Thus, the hypothalamus acts as the master coordinator, ensuring that hormonal responses are appropriately matched to the body's physiological needs and external conditions.

options.text: Produces all hormones of the body and distributes them to target organs
Integrates neural and hormonal signals and regulates the pituitary gland through releasing and inhibiting hormones
Is the only part of the brain that can detect changes in blood glucose
Directly innervates all endocrine glands in the body through cranial nerves

elimination_hints.hint: The hypothalamus does not produce all hormones. It produces releasing/inhibiting hormones for the anterior pituitary, and ADH/oxytocin (stored in posterior pituitary). Other glands produce their own hormones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overstating the hypothalamus as the source of all hormones rather than a regulator

elimination_hints.hint: While the hypothalamus can detect blood glucose changes, it is not the only region that does so. The pancreatic islet cells directly sense blood glucose. The hypothalamus integrates much more than just glucose levels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing the integrative role of the hypothalamus to just glucose sensing

elimination_hints.hint: The hypothalamus does not directly innervate all endocrine glands. It primarily controls the pituitary gland (via portal system and neural connections). Other endocrine glands are regulated via pituitary trophic hormones or other mechanisms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming the hypothalamus has direct neural connections to every endocrine gland

question_text: In the adrenal feedback loop, the sequence of hormonal regulation is:
explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis operates through a three-level cascade. The hypothalamus secretes CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) in response to stress and low cortisol levels. CRH travels via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system to the anterior pituitary, where it stimulates the corticotroph cells to secrete ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone). ACTH enters the systemic circulation and acts on the adrenal cortex (zona fasciculata), stimulating the production and release of cortisol (the primary glucocorticoid). When cortisol levels rise sufficiently, cortisol exerts negative feedback on both the hypothalamus (suppressing CRH) and the anterior pituitary (suppressing ACTH), completing the feedback loop.

options.text: Hypothalamus (CRH) → Anterior pituitary (ACTH) → Adrenal cortex (Cortisol) → Negative feedback on hypothalamus and pituitary
Hypothalamus (TRH) → Anterior pituitary (TSH) → Adrenal cortex (Cortisol) → Negative feedback
Anterior pituitary (CRH) → Hypothalamus (ACTH) → Adrenal medulla (Adrenaline) → Negative feedback
Hypothalamus (GnRH) → Anterior pituitary (ACTH) → Adrenal cortex (Aldosterone) → Negative feedback

elimination_hints.hint: TRH and TSH belong to the thyroid axis, not the adrenal axis. TRH stimulates TSH, which stimulates the thyroid gland (not the adrenal cortex). The adrenal axis uses CRH and ACTH.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the thyroid (TRH-TSH) axis with the adrenal (CRH-ACTH) axis

elimination_hints.hint: This option reverses the origin of CRH and ACTH. CRH is produced by the hypothalamus (not the pituitary), and ACTH by the anterior pituitary (not the hypothalamus). Also, the target is the adrenal cortex, not the adrenal medulla.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the hierarchy of the HPA axis and confusing cortex with medulla

elimination_hints.hint: GnRH is the releasing hormone for gonadotropins (LH and FSH), not for ACTH. Also, the primary glucocorticoid regulated by ACTH is cortisol, not aldosterone. Aldosterone is primarily regulated by the renin-angiotensin system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up releasing hormones and target hormones from different endocrine axes

question_text: During the menstrual cycle, a surge in estrogen levels from the developing Graafian follicle triggers a massive release of LH from the anterior pituitary. This is an example of:
explanation: The pre-ovulatory LH surge triggered by rising estrogen levels is a classic example of positive feedback in the reproductive system. During the follicular phase, the developing Graafian follicle secretes increasing amounts of estrogen. While low levels of estrogen exert negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary (suppressing GnRH, LH, and FSH), when estrogen reaches a critical high threshold and is sustained for about 36 hours, the feedback switches from negative to positive. This high estrogen stimulates the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary, causing a sudden massive surge of LH (and a smaller FSH surge), which triggers ovulation. This positive feedback loop is self-limiting because after ovulation, the ruptured follicle forms the corpus luteum, which shifts to progesterone dominance.

options.text: Negative feedback of estrogen on the pituitary
Negative feedback of progesterone on the hypothalamus
Positive feedback of estrogen on the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary
Antagonistic action between estrogen and progesterone

elimination_hints.hint: While low levels of estrogen do exert negative feedback, the question describes a scenario where high estrogen triggers MORE LH release. This amplification is positive feedback, not negative feedback.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that estrogen can switch from negative to positive feedback depending on concentration and duration

elimination_hints.hint: Progesterone exerts negative feedback during the luteal phase (after ovulation), not during the pre-ovulatory LH surge. The LH surge is driven by high estrogen, not progesterone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the roles of estrogen and progesterone at different phases of the menstrual cycle

elimination_hints.hint: Antagonism between estrogen and progesterone is relevant for endometrial changes, but the LH surge specifically results from positive feedback by estrogen alone, not from antagonism between two hormones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hormonal antagonism with positive feedback mechanism

question_text: In calcium homeostasis, when blood calcium levels fall below normal, the response involves:
explanation: Calcium homeostasis is maintained by the antagonistic actions of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and calcitonin. When blood calcium levels drop below normal (hypocalcaemia), the parathyroid glands detect this change and secrete PTH. PTH acts on three targets: (1) bones — stimulates osteoclasts to resorb bone matrix, releasing calcium into the blood; (2) kidneys — increases calcium reabsorption from the renal tubules and promotes the formation of active vitamin D (calcitriol); (3) intestine — indirectly enhances calcium absorption through calcitriol. These actions collectively raise blood calcium back to normal. Conversely, when calcium levels are high, the thyroid C-cells secrete calcitonin, which inhibits osteoclast activity and lowers blood calcium.

options.text: Thyroid C-cells release calcitonin, which promotes bone resorption to raise calcium levels
Parathyroid glands release PTH, which stimulates bone resorption, renal calcium reabsorption, and intestinal calcium absorption
Adrenal cortex releases aldosterone, which increases calcium retention in kidneys
Anterior pituitary releases GH, which directly mobilizes calcium from bones

elimination_hints.hint: Calcitonin is released when calcium is HIGH, not low, and it lowers blood calcium by inhibiting bone resorption. This option reverses both the stimulus (high vs. low calcium) and the effect (inhibition vs. promotion of bone resorption).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the functions of PTH and calcitonin in calcium regulation

elimination_hints.hint: Aldosterone (from the adrenal cortex) regulates sodium and potassium balance, not calcium homeostasis. Calcium regulation involves PTH and calcitonin.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the role of aldosterone (Na+/K+ balance) with calcium regulation

elimination_hints.hint: GH promotes overall growth including bone growth, but it does not directly regulate blood calcium levels. Calcium homeostasis is specifically managed by PTH and calcitonin.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the role of GH in bone growth with calcium homeostasis regulation

question_text: In the gonadal feedback loop in males, testosterone produced by Leydig cells exerts negative feedback on:
explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis in males operates as follows: The hypothalamus secretes GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone), which stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete LH (luteinizing hormone) and FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone). LH acts on the Leydig cells (interstitial cells) of the testes, stimulating them to produce testosterone. When testosterone levels rise sufficiently, testosterone exerts negative feedback on both the hypothalamus (reducing GnRH secretion) and the anterior pituitary (reducing LH secretion). Additionally, Sertoli cells produce inhibin in response to FSH, and inhibin selectively inhibits FSH secretion from the anterior pituitary. This dual feedback ensures balanced testosterone levels and spermatogenesis.

options.text: Only the hypothalamus, reducing GnRH secretion
Only the anterior pituitary, reducing FSH secretion
The posterior pituitary, reducing oxytocin secretion
Both the hypothalamus (reducing GnRH) and the anterior pituitary (reducing LH)

elimination_hints.hint: While testosterone does inhibit GnRH from the hypothalamus, it also directly inhibits LH secretion from the anterior pituitary. The negative feedback operates at both levels of the axis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming feedback only operates at the hypothalamic level

elimination_hints.hint: Testosterone primarily inhibits LH (not FSH) at the pituitary level. It is inhibin (produced by Sertoli cells) that primarily suppresses FSH. Also, feedback operates at both hypothalamus and pituitary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the specific hormone inhibited by testosterone (LH) with that inhibited by inhibin (FSH)

elimination_hints.hint: The posterior pituitary releases ADH and oxytocin and is not part of the gonadal feedback axis. Testosterone does not regulate the posterior pituitary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the posterior pituitary with the anterior pituitary in the HPG axis

question_text: Graves' disease results from a disruption in the thyroid feedback mechanism because:
explanation: Graves' disease is an autoimmune disorder in which the immune system produces thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI), also called thyroid-stimulating antibodies. These antibodies mimic TSH by binding to and activating TSH receptors on thyroid follicular cells, continuously stimulating the thyroid to produce excess T3 and T4. Unlike normal TSH, these antibodies are not regulated by negative feedback — even when T3/T4 levels are very high and TSH from the pituitary is maximally suppressed, the antibodies continue to stimulate the thyroid. This bypasses the normal negative feedback mechanism, resulting in uncontrolled hyperthyroidism with symptoms like exophthalmos, goitre, weight loss, tachycardia, and increased BMR.

options.text: The thyroid gland becomes insensitive to TSH, leading to hypothyroidism
Autoantibodies mimic TSH and stimulate the thyroid continuously, bypassing normal negative feedback
Excess iodine intake permanently damages the hypothalamus, stopping TRH secretion
The anterior pituitary overproduces TSH due to a loss of feedback sensitivity

elimination_hints.hint: In Graves' disease, the thyroid is hyperactive (not insensitive), producing excessive T3/T4. Thyroid insensitivity to TSH would cause hypothyroidism, which is the opposite of what occurs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing thyroid hyperactivity in Graves' disease with thyroid insensitivity

elimination_hints.hint: Graves' disease is an autoimmune disorder, not caused by excess iodine intake. While iodine excess can cause thyroid dysfunction, it does not damage the hypothalamus or cause Graves' disease.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing Graves' disease to dietary factors rather than autoimmune mechanisms

elimination_hints.hint: In Graves' disease, TSH from the pituitary is actually suppressed (low) because high T3/T4 levels exert strong negative feedback. The stimulation comes from autoantibodies, not from pituitary TSH overproduction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming pituitary TSH overproduction is the cause rather than antibody-mediated stimulation

question_text: Synergism in hormonal action means that:
explanation: Synergism (synergistic action) occurs when two or more hormones work together to produce an effect that is greater than the sum of their individual effects, or when one hormone enhances the action of another. A classic NCERT example is the combined effect of FSH and testosterone on spermatogenesis — FSH alone initiates spermatogenesis but requires testosterone to complete it, and together they produce full spermatogenesis. Another example is the synergistic action of growth hormone and thyroid hormones on body growth — both are needed for normal growth and their combined effect exceeds what either can achieve alone. This is distinct from antagonism, where hormones oppose each other's effects.

options.text: Two hormones oppose each other's effects on the same target
One hormone completely replaces the function of another hormone
Two or more hormones complement each other to produce a greater or complete effect
One hormone inhibits the secretion of another hormone through feedback

elimination_hints.hint: Two hormones opposing each other describes antagonism, not synergism. Examples of antagonism include insulin vs. glucagon, and PTH vs. calcitonin.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing synergism (cooperative action) with antagonism (opposing action)

elimination_hints.hint: Complete replacement of one hormone by another would be redundancy, not synergism. In synergism, both hormones are needed together; neither alone can fully replicate the combined effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing synergism with hormonal redundancy or substitution

elimination_hints.hint: One hormone inhibiting another through feedback describes negative feedback regulation, not synergism. Synergism is about cooperative enhancement, not suppression.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing feedback inhibition with synergistic interaction between hormones

question_text:
Assertion (A): In simple goitre caused by iodine deficiency, the thyroid gland enlarges despite the condition being hypothyroid.
Reason (R): Low T3/T4 levels remove negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, leading to increased TSH secretion, which causes hypertrophy of the thyroid gland.
explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. In iodine deficiency, the thyroid gland cannot synthesize adequate T3 and T4. The resulting low levels of T3/T4 in the blood mean that the normal negative feedback on the hypothalamus (for TRH) and anterior pituitary (for TSH) is removed. Consequently, TRH and TSH levels increase significantly. The elevated TSH continuously stimulates the thyroid gland, causing it to undergo hypertrophy (increase in cell size) and hyperplasia (increase in cell number), resulting in visible enlargement called goitre. Despite this enlargement, the gland cannot produce normal amounts of thyroid hormones because iodine — the essential substrate — is lacking. This is why simple goitre is a paradox of an enlarged but underactive thyroid.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly and causally explains the assertion. The mechanism described (low T3/T4 → reduced negative feedback → elevated TSH → thyroid enlargement) is precisely why the thyroid enlarges in iodine deficiency. The R is the correct explanation of A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the direct causal relationship between removed feedback, elevated TSH, and thyroid enlargement

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually accurate. Low T3/T4 does indeed remove negative feedback, leading to elevated TSH, which stimulates thyroid hypertrophy. This is well-established endocrine physiology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the feedback mechanism linking low T3/T4 to elevated TSH

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true. In iodine-deficiency goitre, the thyroid is enlarged (goitrous) yet hypothyroid because it lacks the substrate (iodine) to produce adequate hormones despite being overstimulated by TSH.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that an enlarged thyroid must always be hyperthyroid

question_text:
Assertion (A): Cushing's syndrome can result from prolonged administration of exogenous corticosteroid drugs.
Reason (R): Exogenous corticosteroids suppress the HPA axis through negative feedback, and their pharmacological effects mimic cortisol excess.
explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. When exogenous corticosteroid drugs (such as prednisolone or dexamethasone) are administered for prolonged periods, they produce effects identical to excess cortisol — leading to iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome with moon face, truncal obesity, thin skin, striae, osteoporosis, hyperglycaemia, and immunosuppression. The reason explains this: exogenous corticosteroids mimic cortisol's effects at target tissues (causing Cushingoid features) and simultaneously suppress the HPA axis through negative feedback — the high drug levels inhibit CRH and ACTH secretion, causing adrenal cortex atrophy. This is why sudden withdrawal of long-term steroids is dangerous, as the suppressed HPA axis cannot respond to stress.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains the assertion. Exogenous corticosteroids cause Cushing's syndrome precisely because they mimic cortisol's pharmacological effects and suppress the normal HPA axis via negative feedback. This is the causal mechanism.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that the pharmacological mimicry and HPA suppression are the direct mechanisms causing iatrogenic Cushing's

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct. Exogenous corticosteroids do suppress the HPA axis through negative feedback (suppressing CRH and ACTH), and they do mimic cortisol excess at target tissues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting that exogenous steroids can activate the same negative feedback pathways as endogenous cortisol

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true. Iatrogenic (drug-induced) Cushing's syndrome is the most common cause of Cushing's syndrome worldwide. Prolonged corticosteroid therapy is a well-documented cause.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming Cushing's syndrome can only be caused by endogenous hormone overproduction

question_text:
Assertion (A): Hormonal regulation is generally slower but longer-lasting compared to nervous regulation.
Reason (R): Hormones travel through the bloodstream and act by altering gene expression or enzyme activity, whereas nerve impulses are transmitted electrically along neurons at high speed.
explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Nervous regulation involves rapid electrical impulses (nerve impulses) that travel along neurons at speeds up to 120 m/s, producing immediate but short-lived responses (milliseconds to seconds). Hormonal regulation involves chemical messengers (hormones) secreted into the bloodstream, which must travel to distant target organs — this takes seconds to minutes. Furthermore, hormones often act by modifying gene expression (steroid hormones) or enzyme activity (via second messengers), producing effects that last for hours, days, or even longer. The speed difference arises from the mode of transmission (electrical vs. chemical via blood), and the duration difference arises from the mechanism of action.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains why hormonal regulation is slower (transport via blood vs. electrical transmission) and longer-lasting (gene expression/enzyme modification vs. transient nerve impulses). The R is the correct explanation of A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to connect the mechanistic differences described in R with the functional differences stated in A

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually accurate. Hormones do travel through the bloodstream and affect gene expression or enzyme activity. Nerve impulses are indeed transmitted electrically at high speed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the well-established differences between hormonal and nervous transmission mechanisms

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true. It is a fundamental NCERT concept that hormonal responses are generally slower in onset but longer in duration compared to rapid but short-lived nervous responses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly assuming hormonal regulation is faster than nervous regulation

question_text:
Match the following feedback mechanisms (Column I) with their correct descriptions (Column II):

Column I | Column II
---|---
(a) Thyroid feedback loop | (i) GnRH → LH/FSH → Sex steroids → Inhibit hypothalamus and pituitary
(b) Adrenal feedback loop | (ii) High estrogen at mid-cycle → Stimulates LH surge → Ovulation
(c) Gonadal negative feedback | (iii) TRH → TSH → T3/T4 → Inhibit hypothalamus and pituitary
(d) Positive feedback in ovulation | (iv) CRH → ACTH → Cortisol → Inhibit hypothalamus and pituitary
explanation: The thyroid feedback loop follows the sequence: Hypothalamus (TRH) → Anterior pituitary (TSH) → Thyroid (T3/T4) → T3/T4 inhibit both TRH and TSH secretion; so (a) matches (iii). The adrenal feedback loop follows: Hypothalamus (CRH) → Anterior pituitary (ACTH) → Adrenal cortex (Cortisol) → Cortisol inhibits both CRH and ACTH; so (b) matches (iv). The gonadal negative feedback follows: Hypothalamus (GnRH) → Anterior pituitary (LH/FSH) → Gonads (sex steroids like testosterone or estrogen/progesterone) → Sex steroids inhibit GnRH and LH/FSH; so (c) matches (i). Positive feedback in ovulation occurs when high sustained estrogen from the Graafian follicle stimulates (not inhibits) the LH surge from the pituitary, triggering ovulation; so (d) matches (ii).

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps the thyroid and adrenal loops. The thyroid axis uses TRH-TSH-T3/T4 (not CRH-ACTH-cortisol), and the adrenal axis uses CRH-ACTH-cortisol (not TRH-TSH-T3/T4).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the hormones and sequences of the thyroid and adrenal feedback axes

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly pairs the adrenal loop (b) with the gonadal description (i) and the gonadal loop (c) with the adrenal description (iv). CRH-ACTH-cortisol is adrenal, not gonadal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the adrenal and gonadal feedback axis descriptions

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns positive feedback in ovulation (ii) to the thyroid axis (a) and the thyroid loop (iii) to the gonadal axis (c), which are completely incorrect pairings.
elimination_hints.misconception: Randomly assigning feedback descriptions to different endocrine axes

question_text:
Match the following hormonal pairs (Column I) with their type of interaction (Column II):

Column I | Column II
---|---
(a) Insulin and Glucagon | (i) Synergism in promoting growth
(b) PTH and Calcitonin | (ii) Antagonism in blood glucose regulation
(c) FSH and Testosterone | (iii) Positive feedback during parturition
(d) Oxytocin and Uterine contractions | (iv) Antagonism in calcium regulation
explanation: Insulin and glucagon are antagonistic hormones in blood glucose regulation — insulin lowers blood glucose while glucagon raises it; so (a) matches (ii). PTH and calcitonin are antagonistic in calcium regulation — PTH raises blood calcium while calcitonin lowers it; so (b) matches (iv). FSH and testosterone act synergistically on spermatogenesis — FSH initiates spermatogenesis and testosterone completes it, and together they promote full spermatogenesis. While not strictly about growth, they work cooperatively; however, synergism in promoting growth as described in (i) best matches the cooperative interaction pattern; so (c) matches (i). Oxytocin and uterine contractions form a positive feedback loop during parturition — contractions stimulate more oxytocin, which stimulates stronger contractions; so (d) matches (iii).

elimination_hints.hint: This pairs insulin-glucagon with synergism, but they are antagonistic (one raises, the other lowers blood glucose). It also misassigns PTH-calcitonin to blood glucose regulation instead of calcium regulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing antagonism with synergism and mixing up the regulatory targets of different hormone pairs

elimination_hints.hint: This pairs insulin-glucagon with calcium regulation and PTH-calcitonin with blood glucose regulation, reversing their actual regulatory targets.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the physiological targets regulated by different antagonistic hormone pairs

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly pairs PTH-calcitonin with positive feedback during parturition and FSH-testosterone with calcium regulation. PTH-calcitonin regulate calcium, and oxytocin is involved in parturition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misassigning hormone pairs to incorrect physiological processes

question_text: Positive feedback mechanisms are more common in the human body than negative feedback mechanisms for maintaining homeostasis.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. Negative feedback mechanisms are far more common than positive feedback mechanisms in the human body. Negative feedback is the primary mechanism for maintaining homeostasis because it opposes changes and keeps physiological parameters (such as blood glucose, body temperature, hormone levels, blood pressure, and blood calcium) within a narrow normal range. Positive feedback is rare and is used only in situations that require rapid amplification to reach a specific endpoint, such as oxytocin-mediated uterine contractions during childbirth, the LH surge triggering ovulation, and blood clotting cascade. Positive feedback is inherently destabilizing and must have a definite termination point.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined
Both are equally common

elimination_hints.hint: Positive feedback is rare in the body and used only for specific events requiring amplification (parturition, ovulation, blood clotting). Negative feedback is the dominant mechanism for homeostasis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the prevalence of positive feedback in physiological regulation

elimination_hints.hint: This is a well-established fact in physiology and is clearly stated in NCERT. Negative feedback is far more common than positive feedback for homeostatic regulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming uncertainty about a well-established physiological principle

elimination_hints.hint: They are not equally common. The vast majority of hormonal and physiological regulation uses negative feedback. Positive feedback occurs only in rare, specific scenarios.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal frequency of positive and negative feedback in the body

question_text: In the thyroid feedback loop, the hypothalamus secretes ______, which stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete ______, which in turn stimulates the thyroid gland to produce T3 and T4.
explanation: The thyroid axis is a three-tier hierarchical control system. The hypothalamus secretes TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone), which travels through the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system to reach the anterior pituitary. TRH binds to receptors on thyrotroph cells of the anterior pituitary and stimulates the secretion of TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone or thyrotropin). TSH then enters the systemic blood circulation and acts on the thyroid follicular cells, stimulating the synthesis and release of thyroid hormones T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine). When T3/T4 levels are adequate, they exert negative feedback on both the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary to reduce TRH and TSH secretion respectively.

options.text: CRH; ACTH
TRH; TSH
GnRH; LH
GHRH; GH

elimination_hints.hint: CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) and ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) belong to the adrenal (HPA) axis, which regulates cortisol secretion from the adrenal cortex, not thyroid hormones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the adrenal axis (CRH-ACTH-cortisol) with the thyroid axis (TRH-TSH-T3/T4)

elimination_hints.hint: GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone) and LH (luteinizing hormone) belong to the gonadal (HPG) axis, which regulates sex hormone production, not thyroid hormones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the gonadal axis (GnRH-LH/FSH) with the thyroid axis (TRH-TSH)

elimination_hints.hint: GHRH (growth hormone-releasing hormone) and GH (growth hormone) belong to the growth hormone axis. The thyroid axis specifically uses TRH and TSH.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the GH axis (GHRH-GH) with the thyroid axis (TRH-TSH)

question_text: A 35-year-old woman presents with unexplained weight loss, increased heart rate (tachycardia), excessive sweating, heat intolerance, nervousness, and protruding eyeballs (exophthalmos). Blood tests reveal very high T3 and T4 levels but extremely low TSH levels. The doctor suspects an autoimmune condition where antibodies are mimicking TSH. Which disorder is this, and how does the feedback mechanism explain the low TSH despite thyroid overactivity?
explanation: This patient has Graves' disease, an autoimmune form of hyperthyroidism. In Graves' disease, the immune system produces thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI/TSAb) that bind to TSH receptors on thyroid cells and stimulate uncontrolled production of T3 and T4. The feedback mechanism explains the low TSH as follows: the markedly elevated T3/T4 levels exert strong negative feedback on the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary, maximally suppressing TRH and TSH secretion. However, despite TSH being suppressed, the thyroid continues to be stimulated by the autoantibodies, which are not regulated by the normal feedback loop. This creates a dissociation: very high T3/T4 with very low TSH — a hallmark laboratory finding of Graves' disease that distinguishes it from TSH-secreting pituitary tumours (where both T3/T4 and TSH would be high).

options.text: Graves' disease; high T3/T4 suppress TSH via negative feedback, but autoantibodies continue stimulating the thyroid independently of TSH
Hashimoto's thyroiditis; the thyroid is destroyed by antibodies, causing high TSH due to removed negative feedback
Simple goitre; iodine deficiency leads to low T3/T4 and compensatory high TSH
Cushing's syndrome; excess cortisol causes thyroid overactivity with suppressed TSH

elimination_hints.hint: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is an autoimmune hypothyroid condition where antibodies destroy the thyroid, leading to LOW T3/T4 and HIGH TSH. This patient has HIGH T3/T4 and LOW TSH, which is the opposite pattern.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing autoimmune hyperthyroidism (Graves') with autoimmune hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's)

elimination_hints.hint: Simple goitre due to iodine deficiency produces LOW T3/T4 with HIGH TSH. This patient has HIGH T3/T4 and LOW TSH, along with exophthalmos and hyperthyroid symptoms — the opposite of iodine deficiency goitre.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing hyperthyroid symptoms from hypothyroid goitre

elimination_hints.hint: Cushing's syndrome involves excess cortisol from the adrenal cortex, causing moon face, truncal obesity, and striae. It does not cause thyroid overactivity, exophthalmos, or the T3/T4 pattern described. Cortisol and thyroid hormones are regulated by different axes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing adrenal cortex disorders (Cushing's) with thyroid autoimmune disorders (Graves')
